architecture.summary: EKI-BART augments BART (pretrained encoder-decoder transformer) with two trainable modules that operate on retrieved prototype sentences. The system concatenates input concepts and a prototype retrieved from external corpora, then adds group embeddings to distinguish concept tokens from prototype tokens. A scaling module learns per-token importance weights applied during encoder-decoder attention, downweighting noise and highlighting scenario-relevant tokens. A position indicator function computes the minimum distance from each prototype token to the nearest concept token, encoding this distance as an additional embedding added to the decoder's attention. An auxiliary classification loss during training encourages the scaling module to predict which prototype tokens will appear in the target output.
trained_components: encoder classification head (auxiliary task for scaling)

biblio.title: An Enhanced Knowledge Injection Model for Commonsense Generation

claims.statement: EKI-BART achieves 24.36 BLEU-2 and 45.42 BLEU-4 on CommonGen test set with in-domain prototype corpus, compared to BART baseline of 22.87 BLEU-2 and 41.44 BLEU-4
EKI-BART achieves 25.43 BLEU-2 and 46.53 BLEU-4 on CommonGen test set with out-of-domain prototype corpus (Wikipedia), compared to BART baseline of 23.15 BLEU-2 and 44.71 BLEU-4
All evaluation metrics show improvement: ROUGE-2/L, BLEU-3/4, METEOR, CIDEr, and SPICE all increase with EKI-BART across in-domain and out-of-domain settings

The scaling module outperforms hard masking strategies (HM1, HM2) because hard masking removes additional effective concepts that contribute to the target scene, whereas soft weighting retains this information while downweighting noise
Adding prototype position indicator to BART+GE+SM yields 0.7 BLEU-3 improvement on development set and 0.8 BLEU-3 improvement on test set (from 44.7 to 45.4 dev; 45.2 to 46.0 test)
Group embedding (GE) that distinguishes concept tokens from prototype tokens improves generation quality, and the combination of GE with scaling module (SM) further enhances performance
In-domain corpora (VaTeX, SNLI, Activity, CommonGen training) retrieve more relevant prototypes than out-of-domain Wikipedia, though out-of-domain retrieval still provides measurable benefit

Retrieve-only baseline (using only retrieved prototypes without editing via BART) performs substantially worse than pretrained models, indicating that retrieval alone is insufficient for commonsense generation
